**HKU Course Project Documentation Tool**

**Course Submission Documentation**

Created by **Jim Tze Lau** as part of BSIM3021 Individual Assignment for HKU 2025-26 Spring Semester

Accessible website: <https://supernoobcraft.github.io/hku-toolkit/>

**Browser Compatibility:** The website is designed for common desktop browsers including Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge, and Firefox. Mobile layouts are responsive and usable for viewing and editing workflows. ZIP import/export requires modern Blob and JSZip support, and local evidence persistence depends on IndexedDB availability.

# Introduction

## HKU-Related Theme and Purpose

The HKU Course Project Documentation Tool addresses fragmented project evidence: HKU students produce substantial coursework (code, reports, prototypes) scattered across platforms (learning management systems, personal storage, team chats). When preparing internship/scholarship applications or capstone reflections, students must reconstruct their work narrative and present clear evidence in a neat portfolio.

**Theme:** Academic project portfolio construction for HKU students, consolidating fragmented outputs into coherent, portable format for professional communication.

## Goals and Objectives

- **Structured Documentation:** Consistent format supporting academic rigor and professional communication
- **Cognitive Load Reduction:** Minimize repeated effort during portfolio preparation
- **Flexible Field Structure:** Required fields (completeness) + optional fields (depth)
- **Portability & Continuity:** Local persistence + ZIP/PDF export workflows
- **Accessible Design:** Usable interface aligned with web principles (Nielsen, 1994; WCAG 2.2)

# Target Users and User Needs Assessment

## User Profile and Characteristics

Primary users: HKU undergraduates/postgraduates summarizing coursework for external audiences.

Key characteristics:

- Deadline-driven (application cycles, exam periods)
- Mixed technical confidence (non-programming to engineering students)
- Narrative fluidity (reorder for audiences)
- Data-loss anxiety (draft work concerns)
- Low-friction preference (minimize steps/decisions).

Secondary users (mentors, employers, admission officers) are also considered, and they prefer informed design for clear English, concise labels, and print-ready format.

## Assessment Methods and Rationale

**Method:** Convenient Sampling through sending questionnaires to HKU classmates.

**Sample:** N=6 (Year 2): Arts (2), Engineering (2), Business (1), Education (1). Project volume: 4/6 with 3-5 projects/semester; 2/6 with 1-2 projects/semester.

## Assessment Results and Design Implications

**Key Findings of User Needs and Fears:**

- File fragmentation problem (4.17/5): Led to ZIP/IndexedDB persistence features
- Data-loss anxiety (5 mentions): Implemented scheduled autosave every 3 minutes, up to 20 past autosave snapshots are saved, and can be manually cleared by users after warning (or by clearing browser data)
- Narrative flexibility needs: Manual reordering by dragging or using accessible directional buttons (↑/↓), or sort automatically (by time, name, etc) under a dropdown menu
- Low-friction preference (4 mentions): only 6 required fields for bare minimum information (course code, name, year, term, type, title), other items are all optional for extra depth
- Post-graduation access (4.00/5): Dual export (ZIP backup, PDF submission)

**Design Principle Integration:** These findings did not dictate the design on their own, but they strongly influenced which features were prioritized. Features were implemented through a Human-Cantered Design approach based on Nielsen's (1994) usability heuristics (user control/freedom via undo and history; visibility via autosave indicator), as well as web authoring principles (less is more, keep your promises).

**Critical Reflection:** Survey (N=6, all Year 2) yields directional rather than representative findings. Convenience sampling may skew toward digitally-engaged students. Results may not generalize to postgraduate or non-technical faculties. Consistent signal strength (3.17-4.17/5 means) justified prioritizing reliability and export-first approaches.

# Website Creation Methods and Tools

## Development Methodology

Iterative feature-driven approach:

- Stable, extensible data model for projects
- Form capture + validation
- Live preview for feedback
- Persistence + recovery mechanisms
- Portability (ZIP/PDF)
- Usability/accessibility refinement

## Technology Stack and Rationale

**Technologies:**

- HTML (semantic structure: fieldset, legend, labels)
- CSS (responsive two-column layout, themes, print styling)
- JavaScript (state management, autosave, history, sorting, import/export)
- JSZip (in-browser ZIP without server)
- GitHub Pages (static deployment, version control)

**Rationale:** Prioritizes transparency and maintainability for coursework review; eliminates server dependencies (reduces operational risk); maximizes creative freedom; supports incremental development and debugging (Fielding, 2000).

## AI-Supported Development Tools

AI-supported tools were used as **development assistance**, not autonomous authority. Practical contributions included:

- **Code Drafting:** Assistance with repetitive JavaScript UI patterns and event-handling logic
- **Debugging Support:** Collaborative problem-solving during state-synchronization and event-flow issues
- **Wording Refinement:** Content optimization for user-facing text, helper content, and error messages
- **Implementation Comparison:** Rapid evaluation of alternative approaches before manual final review

**Critical Approach:** All AI-assisted outputs underwent human validation for correctness, scope alignment, and behavioural consistency.

# Web Usability

## Overview: How usability was approached

Nielsen's (1994) usability heuristics were used as **evaluative lenses** during development rather than rigid rules. In practice, several heuristics proved more influential than others, especially **visibility of system status**, **user control & freedom**, and **error prevention**, because these aligned strongly with the needs identified in the user needs assessment (e.g., data-loss anxiety, low-friction preference, narrative flexibility). These findings did not dictate the design on their own, but they strongly influenced which features were prioritized.

### Linking user needs to usability priorities

Key usability priorities were traced to assessed needs:

- **Data-loss anxiety (5 mentions)** → scheduled autosave every 3 minutes, up to 20 past autosave snapshots, and manual clearing after warning.
- **Low-friction preference (4 mentions)** → only 6 required fields (course code, name, year, term, type, title), with optional fields for depth.
- **Narrative flexibility needs** → manual reordering by dragging or using accessible directional buttons (↑/↓), and sort modes (time, name, etc.) under a dropdown menu.
- **Post-graduation access (4.00/5)** → dual export (ZIP backup, PDF submission)

### Key design decisions and features

**Split-Pane Layout:** Left (project management/editing) right (live preview) split reduces cognitive load by maintaining workflow continuity (Norman, 2013).

![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Image 1: Split-pane layout

**Progressive Disclosure:** Editor hidden until needed, reducing initial complexity and supporting bounded decision-making (Shneiderman, 2010).

![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/png;base64,)

Image 2, 3: Project Editor hidden when not in use, only essential UI visible

**Balanced Required/Optional Fields:** 6 required identity fields ensure context capture; optional sections (narrative, technical, reflection) enable depth without forcing overhead. Addresses need for complete yet flexible workflows.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Image 4: Only items marked with \* are required

**Rich List Organization:** Drag reordering + sort dropdown (7 modes) + directional buttons (↑/↓) support diverse preferences from task walkthroughs.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Image 5: Different options for users to organize their list

**Immediate Feedback:** Status bar, autosave indicator, inline errors implement Nielsen's visibility principle.

![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/png;base64,)

Image 6, 7: Feedback for success/error immediately visible to user

Portability & continuity: Zip import and dual export (ZIP backup, PDF submission) to support long-term access.

![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/png;base64,)

Image 8, 9, 10: Easy import / export workflow

**User Error Prevention: Pop-up warning for deleting project / autosave snapshots, blocks saving when required fields are empty.**

**![](data:image/png;base64,)**![](data:image/png;base64,)

**Image 11, 12: Delete confirmation, invalid save prevention**

**Reversibility:** Undo, history snapshots (up to 20), confirmation dialogs reduce anxiety around irreversible loss (primary concern: 5 mentions in survey).

![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/png;base64,)

Image 13, 14: Undo button and autosave

### How Nielsen's heuristics were applied (mapped to the features above)

Below are the most relevant heuristics and the concrete implementations in the tool.[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)

- **Visibility of system status:** Autosave indicator, status bar messages, confirmation dialogs.[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)
- **Match with real world:** Student vocabulary for presenting projects (Objectives, Deliverables, etc.).[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)
- **User control & freedom:** Undo, manual drag reordering, cancel buttons, explicit confirmations.[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)
- **Error prevention:** Dropdown selectors, field validation, blocks save when required fields empty.[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)
- **Flexibility:** Directional buttons, sort modes, preset templates reduce re-entry.[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)
- **Aesthetic minimalism:** Essential UI visible; advanced features in menus.[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)
- **Help & documentation:** Quick Start Guide on first visit and Help page.[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)

**Web Authoring Principles Applied:**[](https://connecthkuhk-my.sharepoint.com/personal/hoganjim_connect_hku_hk/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B4A4BB2D1-8C87-4E03-B111-9488FB0D9AF0%7D&file=Documentation.docx&action=default&mobileredirect=true)

- Know users (needs assessment informed priorities)
- Prepare sitemap (main tool, about, help)
- Draw wireframe (split-pane layout)
- Plan content (project lifecycle flow)
- Learn from good sites (Notion, Trello patterns)
- Inform vs. impress (clarity over decoration)
- Less is more (6 required fields, optional narrative)
- Keep promises (ZIP/PDF export delivered as advertised)

### Trade-offs and reflection

**Trade-offs:** Requiring 6 identity fields ensures completeness but increases entry effort; optional narrative fields relieve this but depend on user motivation. Progressive disclosure improves initial usability but may hide features from less-experienced users, although mitigated by help page and in-app guidance.

# Web Accessibility

Accessibility is deeply integrated throughout the website, well aligned with WCAG 2.2 principles: **Perceivable** (users perceive interface), **Operable** (keyboard/mouse control), **Understandable** (clear, predictable), **Robust** (assistive tech compatible).

## Implementation by WCAG Principle

**Perceivable:** Color contrast WCAG AA (4.5:1 normal, 3:1 large); descriptive alt text on icons; readable 16px base font with zoom support; clear headings (h1-h3) and whitespace.

**Operable:** Full keyboard access (Tab, Enter/Space, arrow keys to quickly scroll through project items, Escape to close pop-ups); focus-visible outlines exceeding AA standards; no time limits on forms; logical tab order with focus containment in modals; skip link to main content.

**Understandable:** Semantic HTML (fieldset, legend, explicit labels); consistent navigation/layout across pages; helper text with examples ("e.g., 'Students lack study management'"); validation errors explain problem and solution; plain language avoids jargon.

**Robust:** ARIA landmarks (main, nav, aside) for screen readers; live regions (role="status" for messages, role="alert" for errors); custom selects preserve keyboard behaviour and ARIA semantics; explicit form labelling with aria-required="true".

![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/png;base64,)

Image 15, 16: Comparison of Light/Dark Mode

## User Contexts Informing Accessibility

Website is expected to be used in diverse contexts such as desktops, laptops, and mobile devices. Therefore, it is important to make sure the website retains a responsive layout whether a user is using mouse, keyboard, trackpad, and other unconventional input methods.

Users may also vary in degrees of technical confidence, progressive disclosure hiding advanced features allows users to proceed at their level of expertise. Students with disabilities (dyslexia, motor impairments, visual impairments, ADD) are also considered, leading to screen reader compatibility, keyboard-only operation, reduced-motion support, scannable text, among other features.

**Accessibility check note:** WAVE was used for a preliminary scan (AIM Score: 9.6/10 for Light Mode, 8.7/10 for Dark Mode).

**Limitation:** Despite the strong preliminary score, no full third-party audit (Axe + screen-reader user testing) has been completed yet. Edge cases in drag-reorder and vestibular-disorder testing remain future work. Additionally, as I do not personally rely on assistive technologies on a daily basis, these decisions were guided primarily by established guidelines rather than firsthand experience.

# Ethical and Cybersecurity Considerations

## Ethical Considerations

**1\. Data Ownership and User Autonomy:** Client-side architecture (no cloud storage, no accounts) ensures users retain control via local IndexedDB + explicit exports. Reflects informational autonomy principles; reduces barriers for socioeconomically diverse students (Floridi & Taddeo, 2016).

**2\. Transparency in Data Handling:** Privacy policy explicitly explains local storage, no server transmission, export/import workflows. Import overwrite requires confirmation, preventing accidental loss (Peppet, 2014).

**3\. Responsible AI Usage:** AI helped with code patterns, debugging, and wording. The final decisions and quality checks stayed with the author, and the About page notes that clearly.

**4\. Fairness in Skill Representation:** Optional skills summary uses keyword extraction (not AI inference). Users control inclusion/exclusion. Protects against algorithmic misrepresentation in hiring/scholarship contexts (Introna & Nissenbaum, 2000).

**5\. Accessibility as Ethical Obligation:** WCAG alignment reflects commitment to equal access, not compliance checkbox.

## Cybersecurity Awareness

**1\. Injection Prevention:** Text sanitized via .textContent/.appendChild() (not innerHTML), preventing XSS in shared portfolios (OWASP Foundation, 2021).

**2\. Import Trust Boundaries:** ZIP imports validate manifest structure, reject malformed bundles with clear error messages.

**3\. Data Minimization:** No collection of email/profile beyond user-entered project descriptions means no breach surface (McGraw, 2006).

**4\. Storage Isolation:** IndexedDB keys scope-namespaced by course/project identifiers, reducing cross-project leakage on shared origins.

**5\. Local Device Risk (Transparent):** Storage exclusively local means device compromise remains residual risk. Privacy page advises clearing storage on shared computers. Honesty about limitations aligns with threat modeling principles (McGraw, 2006).

**Trade-off Reflection:** Production systems would add CSP headers, rate limiting, audit logging. For educational client-side tool, above measures balance security awareness with feasible implementation. Transparency about limitations is itself responsible practice.

# Limitations and Future Improvements

## Current Limitations

- **User Research Scope (N=6, all Year 2):** Convenience sample via single platform likely skewed toward digitally-engaged students. Postgraduate/low-tech-confidence learners underrepresented. Results directional only.
- **Faculty Inference:** Course-code prefix mapping (e.g., "COMP" → Engineering) cannot accommodate all course variations or interdisciplinary offerings. Fallback to manual input creates friction.
- **Accessibility Testing:** Designed per WCAG principles but no formal third-party audit (WAVE, Axe) or testing with real assistive-technology users completed. Drag-reorder and vestibular-disorder edge cases remain untested.
- **Import Merge:** ZIP import is replacement-only; conflict resolution unavailable. Multi-device portfolio updates require manual reconciliation.
- **Skills Extraction:** Keyword-based matching misses domain terminology not in predefined list (e.g., "reinforced concrete beam" → "beam" not recognized as civil engineering skill).
- **PDF Rendering:** Output quality varies across browsers/platforms; desktop printing remains superior for professional documents.
- **Cross-Device Access:** Manual export/import required; no automatic synchronization (intentional for privacy). Creates friction for iterative work.

## Future Improvements

- **Merge Mode:** Conflict-resolution dialogs for non-destructive multi-device portfolio updates
- **Expanded Research:** N=15-20 participants across year levels and faculties
- **WCAG Verification:** Formal audits + user testing with assistive-technology users
- **Keyboard Reordering:** Cut/paste alternatives to support non-mouse workflows
- **Rich Metadata:** Caption, tagging, and semantic-schema support (JSON-LD)
- **Encrypted Backup:** Password-protected local packages for additional security assurance
- **Privacy-Preserving Analytics:** Opt-in usage diagnostics without identifying data
- **Course-Code API Integration:** Community-maintained mapping or HKU public catalogue connection

**Trade-off:** Current version prioritizes reliability and export robustness; future iterations should expand accessibility verification (highest user impact) and research depth.

Word Count: 2152

# References

Fielding, R. T. (2000). _Architectural styles and the design of network-based software architectures_. University of California, Irvine.

Floridi, L., & Taddeo, M. (2016). What is data ethics? _Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A: Mathematical, Physical and Engineering Sciences_, _374_(2083), 20160360. <https://doi.org/10.1098/rsta.2016.0360>

Introna, L. D., & Nissenbaum, H. (2000). Shaping the web: Why the politics of search engines matters. _The Information Society_, _16_(3), 169-185. <https://doi.org/10.1080/01972240050133634>

McGraw, G. (2006). _Software security: Building security in_. Addison-Wesley Professional.

Nielsen, J. (1994). _10 usability heuristics for user interface design_. Nielsen Norman Group. <https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/>

Norman, D. A. (2013). _The design of everyday things_ (Revised and expanded ed.). Basic Books.

OWASP Foundation. (2021). _OWASP top 10: The ten most critical web application security risks_. <https://owasp.org/Top10/>

Peppet, S. R. (2014). Regulating the Internet of Things: First steps toward managing discrimination, privacy, security, and consent. _Texas Law Review_, 93, 85-176.

Rubin, J., & Chisnell, D. (2008). _Handbook of usability testing: How to plan, design, and conduct effective tests_ (2nd ed.). Wiley.

Shneiderman, B. (2010). _Designing the user interface: Strategies for effective human-computer interaction_ (5th ed.). Pearson.

World Wide Web Consortium. (2023). _Web content accessibility guidelines (WCAG) 2.2_. <https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/>

## Appendix

- 1. Sample output can be found on <https://supernoobcraft.github.io/CourseProjects.pdf>.
  - The Course Project Documentation Tool is hosted under Jim Tze Lau's personal website, the top left navigation "hamburger menu" leads to other pages created by Jim Tze Lau and is not related to the course assignment.
  - The full GitHub Repository of the website including the unrelated parts can be found on <https://github.com/SuperNoobCraft/supernoobcraft.github.io>.
